title: SEGMENT 12 — Hybrid Fc glycoengineering — designing for multiple attributes

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Hybrid Fc glycoengineering — designing for multiple attributes
Up to now we've been talking about Fc glycan modifications as single-attribute engineering.
Afucose for ADCC.
Galactose for CDC.
Sialic acid for anti-inflammation.
But of course, the Asn297 glycan is one structure, and the different modifications interact.
You can have an antibody that is afucosylated and highly galactosylated, hitting ADCC and CDC simultaneously.
You can have one that is sialylated and afucosylated, which is unusual and produces interesting effects.
The full design space is multi-attribute, and modern glycoengineering increasingly aims at hybrid designs that optimize multiple effector functions at once.
Here's a worked example.
Suppose you are designing an anti-CD20 antibody intended to maximize tumor cell killing in a B-cell lymphoma.
The relevant killing mechanisms include ADCC (NK-mediated), CDC (complement-mediated), and ADCP (macrophage-mediated phagocytosis).
What's your ideal Fc glycoform?
You want high afucosylation, to maximize NK ADCC.
You want high galactosylation, to maximize C1q binding and complement-mediated killing.
You probably want low sialylation, because sialylation can dampen complement engagement and may add an anti-inflammatory signal you don't want.
You want a clean biantennary structure without unusual modifications that might trigger immunogenicity.
In short: G2 (no fucose, two galactoses, no sialic acid).
Producing antibody with a defined G2 glycoform is achievable but requires careful engineering.
You need a FUT8-knockout CHO line (or equivalent) for the afucose.
You need media optimization — manganese supplementation, galactose feeding — to drive galactosylation high.
You may need additional cell-line engineering to suppress sialyltransferase activity if it's adding unwanted sialic acid.
And you need analytics — HPLC-MS, which we'll cover in Segment 15 — to monitor that you're achieving the target glycoform distribution.
Several biotechs have built platforms specifically around multi-attribute glycoengineering.
GlycoNex, Glycotope, Glycos Biotechnologies, and others have all positioned themselves as makers of antibodies with engineered glycoforms beyond just afucose.
Some of these have produced clinical-stage drugs; the field is real but still maturing.
There's also the option of enzymatic remodeling post-production, which I'll mention here briefly and return to in Segment 18.

You can produce an antibody in conventional CHO, harvest it, and then use a panel of glycosyltransferases — recombinantly produced enzymes — to trim the natural glycan back and rebuild it to a defined structure.
This is chemoenzymatic glycoengineering, pioneered by Lai-Xi Wang and others, and it gives you molecule-by-molecule control over the glycoform with no cell-line engineering required.
The cost is that it's a downstream processing step, with associated cost and complexity.
But the precision is unmatched.
The lesson from this segment is design philosophy.
Modern Fc engineering is not "afucose yes or no.

" It is a multidimensional design problem: what effector functions do you want, what receptors do you want to engage and avoid, what conformational dynamics do you want in the CH2 domain, and what manufacturing route will reliably deliver the chosen glycoform distribution.
The Asn297 glycan is the primary lever.
Different modifications tune different effects.
And the good antibody designers think about the whole glycoform together, not just one sugar at a time.
